svg.title: How the nearest-neighbor G-function describes spatial point patterns
svg.desc: A three-step scientific diagram explaining the spatial G-function. First, directed arrows measure the distance from every observed point to its nearest neighbor; nearest-neighbor relationships need not be mutual. Second, all point-specific nearest-neighbor distances are compared with a threshold r, and the fraction no greater than r gives the empirical G-function. Third, the observed G-function is compared with a simulation envelope for a homogeneous planar Poisson process, whose theoretical function is one minus exponential of negative lambda pi r squared. Departures above or below the envelope indicate unusually many or few short nearest-neighbor distances at those spatial scales.

Here, λ is the point intensity.
Naive empirical estimate: Ĝ(r) = #{dᵢ ≤ r} / n
Use edge correction and simulation envelopes for inference.

Spatially varying intensity can mimic clustering;
choose a null model appropriate to the study.
